inference_unit.notations: - $BaCu_{3}O_{4}$: Barium copper oxide with a 1:3:4 stoichiometry. - $T_{c}$: Superconducting transition temperature. - $R$: Electrical resistance. - $T$: Temperature. - $YBa_{2}Cu_{3}O_{7-x}$: Yttrium barium copper oxide, a high-temperature superconductor. - $LaBa_{2}Cu_{3}O_{7-x}$: Lanthanum barium copper oxide, a high-temperature superconductor.

premises.premise: The compound $BaCu_{3}O_{4}$ possesses the lowest melting point among all barium-copper oxide (Ba-Cu-O) phases. [12]
In its liquid state, $BaCu_{3}O_{4}$ exhibits extremely high fluidity (low viscosity). [12]
premises.assumption: Chemical reactions occurring between a melt phase and a solid phase proceed more completely and efficiently toward equilibrium than typical solid-state sintering reactions between purely solid precursors.
premises.premise: The onset temperature of a superconducting transition is defined as the temperature at which the rate of change of the curvature (the second derivative) in the electrical resistance ($R$) versus temperature ($T$) plot, $d^{2}R/dT^{2}$, is maximal. [13]

premise.title: Effect of Calcium substitution in Y- and La-based superconductors

premises.premise: The substitution of 50% of barium atoms with calcium atoms in the superconducting compound $YBa_{2}Cu_{3}O_{7-x}$ results in a depression of the superconducting transition temperature, whereas an equivalent 50% substitution of calcium for barium in the compound $LaBa_{2}Cu_{3}O_{7-x}$ results in an increase in the superconducting transition temperature. [13]
